---
title: | 
    | Introduction and Potential Outcomes
author: "Ryan T. Moore"
date: 2026-07-20
date-format: iso
execute: 
  echo: true
format: 
  beamer:
    fonttheme: serif
    include-in-header:
      - text: | 
          \usepackage{wasysym} 
      - file: zzz_beamer_header.tex
    section-titles: true
    slide-level: 2
    toc: true
institute:
  - American University
bibliography: "../admin/main.bib"
---
  
  
```{r knitr options, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Encoding required for proper knitr rendering
options(encoding = "native.enc")
```

```{r}
#| label: enviro
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(grid)
library(gridExtra)
library(knitr)
library(png)
library(tidyverse)
```

# Welcome

<!-- Include screenshot: -->
<!-- ```{r fig.width=4.4, echo=FALSE} -->
<!-- img <- readPNG("figs/viz-practice.png") -->
<!-- grid.raster(img) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- Include a pdf fig: -->

<!-- \begin{center} -->
<!-- \includegraphics[height=3.1in]{figs/viz-facet-MedNoneRace.pdf} -->
<!-- \end{center} -->

## About Me

\Large

- Associate Prof of Government  
(American University)
- Associate Director, Center for Data Science  
(American University)
- Senior Social Scientist  
(The Lab @ DC)
- (Fellow in Methodology)
(US Office of Evaluation Sciences: "OES") \pause 
- 2021-22: Visiting Fellow  
(Institut Barcelona d'Estudis Internacionals) \pause 
- Research agenda: political methodology, causal inference, experimental design, 
experiments in public policy

## About You!

\Large

>- Name?
>- Role? 
>- Interests?
>- A hobby or non-academic interest?

## The Course: Learning Objectives

\large

>- Identify causal effects using the potential outcomes framework
>- Perform design-based inference for randomized experiments
>- Create and analyze variety of randomized designs, including for blocked, 
conjoint, list, and multiarm bandit experiments
>- Relate experiments to regression quantities
>- Estimate mediation effects and assess their sensitivity
>- Identify appropriate designs for causal inference in observational data
<!-- implement them, and interpret them; these include matching, instrumental  -->
<!-- variables, regression discontinuities, and synthetic controls -->
<!-- >- Assess the sensitivity of estimates to unmeasured confounders -->

\pause 

>- https://github.com/ryantmoore/ci-obs-essex-2026/
>- (Walk through syllabus)


# What is Causal Inference?

## What is Causal Inference?

\Large

\begin{center}
``What caused the terror attacks of 9/11?''
\end{center}

\begin{center}
vs.
\end{center}

\begin{center}
``What is the effect of foreign policy $X$ on domestic terror attacks?''
\end{center}

\pause 
\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
``Causes of an effect''
\end{center}

\begin{center}
vs.
\end{center}

\begin{center}
``Effects of a cause''
\end{center}

## What is Causal Inference?

\LARGE

We will focus on "effects of a cause"\pause, where the "cause" is well-defined.


## Example: Canvassing and Program Enrollment

\pause 

\Large

"Canvassing": a systematic program where agents talk with residents (knock on doors) at the residents' home.

\vspace{1mm}

![Credit: The Campaign Workshop](figs/01-canvass.jpg){width=70%}

## Example: Canvassing and Programme Enrollment

\large

Suppose we ask, ``Would a canvassing policy increase enrollment in a health insurance programme?\text{''} \pause 

\vspace{2mm}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
Citizen & Canvassed? & Enrolled? \\ \hline
1 & Yes & Yes \\
2 & Yes & Yes \\
3 & No & No \\
4 & No & No
\end{tabular}
\end{center} \pause 

\vspace{2mm}

>- What fraction enroll under canvassing vs. no canvassing?  
>- $\frac{2}{2} - \frac{0}{2} = 1$
>- (For each person canvassed, expect 1 more enrollment.)

>- Did the policy "work" (cause more enrollment)?

## Motivating Example: Canvassing and Enrollment

\LARGE
But, is it causal? \newline \pause 
\vspace{1mm}

What do we really want to know? \newline   \pause 
\vspace{1mm}

Would canvassing actually _change_ anyone's enrollment? \newline \pause 

\vspace{1mm}

What would have happened under _other_ conditions? 



## Example: Canvassing and Enrollment

\large 
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
 & & Would Enroll if & Would Enroll if  & \\
Citizen & Canvass? & Canvass? & No Canvass? & Enroll \\ \hline
1 & Yes & \only<2->{Yes} & \only<6->{\alert{(Yes)}} &  Yes \\
2 & Yes & \only<3->{Yes} & \only<6->{\alert{(No)}} &  Yes \\
3 & No & \only<6->{\alert{(Yes)}} & \only<4->{No} &  No \\
4 & No &\only<6->{\alert{(No)}} & \only<5->{No} &  No
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{5mm}

\pause 

\onslide<7->{What is the true causal effect of canvassing? \\}
\onslide<8->{What fraction enroll under canvass vs. no canvass? \\} 
\onslide<9->{What fraction change enrollment due to canvass?\\} 
\onslide<10->{$\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$}


## Example: Canvassing and Enrollment

Empirical data consistent with *different* _unobserved_ outcomes:

\vspace{4mm}

\large
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
 & & Would Enroll if & Would Enroll if  & \\
Citizen & Canvass? & Canvass? & No Canvass? & Enroll \\ \hline
1 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(Yes)} &  Yes \\
2 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(No)} &  Yes \\
3 & No & \alert{(Yes)} & No &  No \\
4 & No & \alert{(Yes)} & No &  No
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

What is the true causal effect of canvass? \newline  
What fraction enroll under canvass vs. no canvass? \newline  \pause 
$\frac{4}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$


## Example: Canvassing and Enrollment

\LARGE

\begin{center}
Well \ldots~ how do we know which?
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never know.
\end{center}

***

\LARGE

\begin{center}
Can we know for one person?
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never know.
\end{center}

***

\LARGE

\begin{center}
But I have some ideas.
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We could not canvass, then canvass later.
\end{center}

\pause  

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never know.
\end{center}


***

\LARGE

\begin{center}
We can never observe both ``Canvassed'' and ``Not Canvassed'' for a unit.
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never observe both \\ \it{potential outcomes}.
\end{center}

\pause  

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never observe both the \\ factual and the counterfactual.
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{5mm}

\begin{center}
We can never know.
\end{center}

## The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference

\LARGE

\begin{center}
We can never observe more than one {\it potential outcome} for a given unit.
\end{center}


***

\LARGE

\begin{center}
So, how can we get a {\it causal} estimate?
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{6mm}

\begin{center}
We infer missing potential outcomes.
\end{center}

# Correlation Is Not Causation

## "Correlation Does Not Mean Causality"

\Large

Common refrain. What does it mean? \pause

Sometimes there is **no** causal relationship **despite** correlation \pause

\vspace{4mm}

>- When the rooster crows, the sun soon rises
>- The rooster did **not** cause the sunrise
>- Had the farmer's cat eaten the rooster, the sun rises anyway

\pause
\vspace{3mm}

Correlation is not enough for causality.

\pause
\vspace{3mm}

\small
(For more, please enjoy https://www.tylervigen.com/spurious-correlations and "spurious scholar")

\normalsize

## Causation Without Correlation

\large

Sometimes there **is** causal relationship and **no** correlation \pause

\vspace{3mm}

A sailor crosses a windy lake:

>- Wind blows; sailor turns rudder to _exactly_ offset it
>- Rudder: back and forth --- yet boat runs **straight**

\pause

\vspace{3mm}

Naïve analyst: "No causal effect of rudder use on path". Rudder does "nothing"! \pause

\vspace{2mm}

But rudder _causes_ straight path

## Causation Without Correlation

\large

What if instead she moved the rudder by a **coin flip**? \pause

\vspace{3mm}

>- Heads: turn left. Tails: turn right.
>- Now the boat **zigzags** across the lake
>- Now the onlooker _sees_ the rudder$\to$heading relationship!

\pause

\vspace{4mm}

\begin{center}
Why visible under randomisation but not otherwise?
\end{center}

\pause

Because the sailor _endogenously_ moves the rudder in response to the
(unobserved) wind. \pause The **randomness** helps reveal the (noisy) causal effect.


## Optimisation Makes Behaviour Endogenous

\Large

Two more examples of optimising behaviour hiding a real effect:

\vspace{3mm}

>- **Central bank** sees a recession forming and buys bonds to head it off.
If done well, see _no_ correlation between its actions and output ---
even though the policy worked.

\pause

>- **Student** who expects to gain most from course is one who enrols. Comparing enrollees to non-enrollees confounds _effect_ of
course with _who chose_ it.

## How This Motivates the Course

\Large

>- Experiments _randomise_ --- choice (Tr status) doesn't depend on potential outcomes
>- Observational data almost never does this
>- This course: **designs** and analyses that recover causal effects from randomised experiments (and approximations)
>- (For more observational designs treatment, see ESS 1I!)

\pause

\vspace{4mm}

\begin{center}
Next, when our naïve estimate works \end{center}

# When Our Naïve Estimate Works 

## Why didn't we recover truth?

\large

The problem with our naïve estimate of effect:

>- "Canvass" group $\neq$ ``No Canvass\text{''} group
>- The _potential outcomes_ help predict whether Canvass!

\pause 

\large
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
 & & Would Enroll if & Would Enroll if  & \\
Citizen & Canvass? & Canvass? & No Canvass? & Enroll \\ \hline
1 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(Yes)} &  Yes \\
2 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(No)} &  Yes \\
3 & No & \alert{(Yes)} & No &  No \\
4 & No &\alert{(No)} & No &  No
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\pause 

\vspace{2mm}

Knowing whether would enroll under canvass _predicts_ whether canvassed!

\pause 
$Pr(\text{Canvass} = \text{Yes} | \text{Would if Canvassed} = \text{Yes}) = \frac{2}{3}$  

\pause 
But 
$Pr(\text{Canvass} = \text{Yes} | \text{Would if Canvassed} = \text{No}) = 0$

## When comparing two groups **does** recover truth

\large

Here, potential outcomes do **not** help predict Canvass:

\large
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
 & & Would Enroll if & Would Enroll if  & \\
Citizen & Canvass? & Canvass? & No Canvass? & Enroll \\ \hline
1 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(Yes)} &  Yes \\
2 & Yes & Yes & \alert{(No)} &  Yes \\
3 & No & \alert{(Yes)} & Yes &  Yes \\
4 & No &\alert{(Yes)} & No &  No
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\pause 

Knowing whether enroll if canvass not predictive.

\pause 

>- What is the _true_ causal effect of canvass?  
>- $\frac{4}{4} - \frac{2}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$
>- What would we _observe_ as the effect of canvass?
>- $\frac{2}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$

\pause 

Good! \smiley



## When does comparing groups recover truth?


<!-- Formally, $Y_1, Y_0 \indep T$, or $E(Y_1, Y_0) = E(Y_1, Y_0 | T)$) -->

\Large

Neither _potential outcome_ should help predict treatment/intervention.  \newline  \pause 

\vspace{4mm}

True underlying responses in "Canvass" group $=$ 
True underlying responses in "No Canvass" group

***

\Large 

(Note: _observed_ outcomes can predict treatment.)  \newline \pause

That's the goal of an intervention!  \pause 

- Aspirin $\implies$ headache!  
- Canvass $\implies$ turnout!  
- Insurance $\implies$ health spending! 
- CBT $\implies$ remission! 

\pause 

But, full schedule of true underlying responses should **not** predict treatment.



## When does comparing groups recover truth?

\LARGE

How to ensure potential outcomes won't predict treatment?\newline  \pause 

\vspace{4mm}

How to _assign_ treatment so it won't predict potential outcomes?


## When does comparing groups recover truth?

\large

Possible _assignment mechanisms_:  

\pause 

\begin{itemize}
\item Let Citizens decide whether to get Canvass \pause 
\item[] (But, those who choose Canvass will Enroll anyway) \pause 
\item[] (We will estimate {\it too large} effect)
\item Let Party decide whom gets Canvass \pause 
\item[] (But, Party will only Canvass those it will affect) \pause
\item[] (We will estimate {\it too large} effect) \pause 
\item What if you {\it randomly} select whom gets Canvass? \pause 
\item[] (``Citizen got Canvass'' won't help guess pot. out.) \pause \item[] (We will estimate {\it correct} effect)
\end{itemize}

# The Potential Outcomes Model


## What is Causal Inference?

\large

>- ``Effects of causes\text{''}

\pause 
\vspace{5mm}

> The objective is to determine for some population of units \ldots the 'typical' causal effect of the [treatment vs. control conditions] on a dependent variable $Y$.
\hspace{80mm} ---Rubin (1974)

\pause 

>- A "causal effect" is a comparative statement

## What is Causal Inference?

![Ramsey & Schafer, _The Statistical Sleuth_](figs/02-grid.pdf)


## What is Causal Inference?

\Large

>- Central definition for causal inference:  
\quad ``a _well-defined_ treatment\text{''}
>- Units must be ``potentially exposable\text{''} to treatment
>- ``No causation without manipulation\text{''}
>- Timing of treatment: outcomes vs. covariates
>- Exclusivity of treatment (to unit)
>- Excludability (assignment effect only via Tr)
>- One study, one causal effect (roughly)



## The Potential Outcomes Model: Ideas

\Large

>- _Unit_: a particular case at a point in time
>- _Treatment_: (putatively) causal variable of interest
>- _Potential outcome_: outcome that would obtain if unit were to receive tr condition
>- _Assignment mechanism_: means by which units come to be sorted into conditions

## The Potential Outcomes Model: Ideas

\Large

Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA)

>- No versions of the treatment, varying in effectiveness
>- No interference between units


## The Potential Outcomes Model: Notation

\Large

>- Units: index $i \in \{1, \ldots, 2n\}$
>- Binary treatment: $T_i \in \{0, 1\}$


>- Pot outcome for $i$ under $T_i = 1$: $Y_{i1}$ or $Y_i(1)$
>- Pot outcome for $i$ under $T_i = 0$: $Y_{i0}$ or $Y_i(0)$

>- For vector of $Y_{i1} \quad \forall i$, write $Y_1$

## The Potential Outcomes Model: Notation

\large 

Below, I'll use the $$\overline{\text{overline}}$$ to represent the average of a _process_ 
-- the expectation. @gergre12 will use $E()$.

\pause 

E.g, the expected value of rolling a fair die is 3.5.  

\pause 

If I roll 4 times and get 2, 6, 1, 1, the sample average is 2.5.  

\pause 

But the expected value of the process is still 3.5.

## The Potential Outcomes Model: Notation

\large 

Observed outcomes

>- The observed outcome: $$Y_i = Y_i(1)\cdot T_i + Y_i(0)\cdot (1 - T_i)$$ \pause  
(sometimes written $Y_i^{obs}$) \pause 
>- If $T_i = 1$, $$Y_i = Y_i(1) \cdot 1 + Y_i(0)(1 - 1) = Y_i(1)$$
>- If $T_i = 0$, $$Y_i = Y_i(1) \cdot 0 + Y_i(0)(1 - 0) = Y_i(0)$$

\pause 

\vspace{-1mm}

The assignment mechanism _selects_ which potential outcome we observe.

## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

Statistical language 

>* _parameter_: unknown numeric value characterizing feature of
  prob model (Greek; $\theta$, $\beta$) 
>* _statistic_: quantity calculable from observed data.  A function. (Roman)
>* _estimator_: statistic used to approximate/guess parameter ($\hat{\theta}$, $\hat{\beta}$)
>* _estimand_: the parameter an estimator attempts to estimate 
>* _estimate_: application of an estimator func to some obs data

\pause 
``The sample statistic $\bar{x}$ is an estimator of true mean
param $\mu$".  

\pause 

$\mu$ is my estimand.  5.1 is my estimate.

## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

\large

For $i$, individual treatment effect

$$\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)$$

>- True treatment effect?
>- Estimate?
>- Observable?

\pause 

\vspace{3mm}

\begin{center}
We can never know.
\end{center}

<!-- ## Random variables and the Expectation -->

<!-- \large -->

<!-- _Random variable_ $X$ is a function mapping sample space to set of reals: $$X:\Omega \to \mathbb{R}$$  -->

<!-- Random variables  -->

<!-- >- summarize outcome of probabilistic/stochastic trial -->
<!-- >- take numerical values.   -->

<!-- \pause  -->

<!-- E.g.,  -->

<!-- >- Let $X =$ number of heads in 3 coin flips.  Is $X$ a random variable? -->
<!-- >- Let $Y$ be outcome of two coin flips, $Y \in \{HH, TH, HT, TT\}$.  Is $Y$ a random variable? -->


<!-- ## Expectation of a Random Variable -->

<!-- The _expected value_ or _expectation_ of a random variable is mean of its outcomes, weighted by their probabilities.  For a discrete random variable, $$E(X) = \sum\limits_{i=1}^n x_ip(x_i)$$ For continuous random variable, $$E(X) = \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} xp(x) dx$$ -->

<!-- Expectation is **not** the sample mean from particular instantiation, a particular data set.  We use sample mean $\bar{x}$ to _estimate_ the expected value. -->

<!-- ## Variance of a Random Variable -->

<!-- The _variance_ of a random variable is mean of outcomes' squared deviations from expectation, weighted by their probabilities: -->

<!-- \begin{eqnarray*} -->
<!-- V(X) & = & E[(X - E(X))^2]\\	 -->
<!-- & = & E(X^2) - (E(X))^2	 -->
<!-- \end{eqnarray*} -->


<!-- ## Properties of Expectation -->

<!-- * $E(c) = c$  -->
<!-- * $E(a+bX) = a+bE(X)$   -->
<!-- * $E(X+Y) = EX + EY$ -->
<!-- * If $X$ and $Y$ indep., then $E(XY) = E(X)E(Y)$ -->

<!-- ## Properties of Variance -->

<!-- * $Var(X) = E(X-EX)^2$  -->
<!-- * $Var(X) = E(X^2)-(EX)^2$  -->
<!-- * $Var(c) = 0$ -->
<!-- * $Var(Y|X) = E(Y^2|X)-(E(Y|X))^2$  -->
<!-- * If $X$ and $Y$ indep., then $Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)$  -->
<!-- * If $X$ and $Y$ indep, then $Var(X-Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)$  -->




## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

>- Average treatment effect
	\begin{displaymath}
	ATE = \overline{Y_1 - Y_0} = \overline{Y_1} - \overline{Y_0}	
	\end{displaymath}

>- Average treatment effect for the treated
	\begin{displaymath}
	ATT = (\overline{Y_1} | T = 1) - (\overline{Y_0} | T = 1)
	\end{displaymath} 


## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

The average treatment effect (ATE):

\begin{eqnarray*}
\overline{\tau} = \overline{TE} = ATE &\equiv & \overline{Y_1 - Y_0} \\
& = & \frac{1}{2n} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{2n} \left( Y_{i1} -
  Y_{i0} \right) \\ \pause
& = & \frac{1}{2n} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{2n} \left( Y_{i1} \right) - \frac{1}{2n} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{2n} \left( Y_{i0} \right) \\ \pause  
& = & \overline{Y_1} - \overline{Y_0} 	%\\ \pause
%& = & \frac{E(Y_1 | T_i =1 ) + E(Y_1 | T_i =0 )}{2} - \frac{E(Y_0 | T_i = 1) + E(Y_0 | T_i = 0)}{2}
\end{eqnarray*} 

\pause 

>- True?
>- Estimated?
>- Observable?


## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

\Large

Statistical Independence \pause 

Define 

$$ Y \independent X$$ 
as

$$Pr(Y | X) = Pr(Y)$$  

\pause 

(Knowing $X$ doesn't change probability of $Y$)


## The Potential Outcomes Model: Estimands

Something we **can** calculate:

If we know $(Y_1, Y_0)$ indep of $T$ \pause \newline
(i.e., knowing true underlying response does **not** indicate $T$) \pause 

Then,

\pause 

\begin{eqnarray*}
\overline{Y_1} & = & (\overline{Y_1} | T = 1) \\ 
\overline{Y_0} & = & (\overline{Y_0} | T = 0)	
\end{eqnarray*}

\pause 

Then, can substitute 

\begin{eqnarray*}
	ATE & = & \overline{Y_1} - \overline{Y_0} \\
	& = & (\overline{Y_1} | T = 1) - (\overline{Y_0} | T = 0)
\end{eqnarray*}

\pause 

Observed diff in Tr and Co group means gives $\overline{TE}$!

## Why Experiments?

\large

Random assignment of treatment promotes 

$$(Y_1, Y_0) \independent T$$ \pause 

When $(Y_1, Y_0)$ and $T$ independent, we can substitute things we know

$$(\overline{Y_1} | T = 1) \text{ and } (\overline{Y_0} | T = 0)$$ \pause 

for things we can _never_ know

$$\overline{Y_1} \text{ and } \overline{Y_0}$$







## Common Assumptions, Null Hyp's in Causal Inference

\large

\begin{itemize}
\item Constant effect:

$$\tau_i = Y_{i1}-Y_{i0} = \tau \quad \forall i$$
\pause 

\item Null hypothesis of no average effect:

$$ATE = \overline{\tau} = 0$$
\pause 

\item Sharp null hypothesis of no effect: 

$$\tau_i = 0$$
\end{itemize}


## Compliance with Treatment Assignment

\note{Rubin's {\bf principal strata}}

\large

Sometimes, units don't follow assignment!

\pause 
\vspace{3mm}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
Assigned Tr & Assigned Co & Type \\ \hline
Tr & Co 	&  Complier \\  \pause 
Tr & Tr & Always-taker \\ \pause 
Co & Co & Never-taker \\ \pause 
Co & Tr & Defier 
\end{tabular}
\end{center}



## Compliance with Treatment Assignment

\large

Of course, we cannot observe $i$ assigned both Tr and Co.

\pause 
\vspace{3mm}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{cccc}
& & \multicolumn{2}{c}{Treatment Taken} \\
& & Treatment & Control \\ \cline{3-4}
Tr Assigned & Tr & Complier/Always & Never/Defier \\
& Co & Always/Defier & Complier/Never \\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}


## Common Estimates under Noncompliance

Let $T_i$ be treatment _assigned_, $D_i$ be treatment _received_.

\pause 

>- Intent-to-treat effect (as-assigned)
	\begin{eqnarray*}
	ITT &=& (\overline{Y_1} | T = 1) - (\overline{Y_0} | T = 0) \\
	&=& (\overline{Y_1} | T = 1, D(T=1)) - (\overline{Y_0} | T = 0, D(T=0)) 
	\end{eqnarray*} 
>- As-treated effect
	\begin{eqnarray*}
	ASTRE &=& (\overline{Y_1} | D = 1) - (\overline{Y_0} | D = 0)
	\end{eqnarray*}


# Computing 

## Computing

\Large

>- R for simulations, estimation, graphics
>- RStudio, an IDE for R
>- GitHub to store materials, submit PS
<!-- >- Calend.ly for scheduling a meeting  -->
>- How should we chat? (email, Slack, \ldots)
<!-- >- RMarkdown for literate programming (papers with data analysis embedded) -->
<!-- >- Slack for course announcements, direct messages, etc. -->
<!-- >- Blackboard for distributing, collecting PS's -->


<!-- ## RMarkdown -->

<!-- \large -->

<!-- We will use RMarkdown, which uses \LaTeX\ for _literate programming_ -- integration of code and English that's output-ready  -->

<!-- \pause  -->

<!-- We will use new R package `tinytex` to convert RMarkdown to PDF files. -->


<!-- ## What RMarkdown Looks Like -->

<!-- ```{r fig.height=3.1, echo=FALSE} -->
<!-- img <- readPNG("figs/00-rmd_raw.png") -->
<!-- grid.raster(img) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ## What RMarkdown Output Looks Like -->

<!-- ```{r fig.height=3.1, echo=FALSE} -->
<!-- img <- readPNG("figs/00-rmd_render.png") -->
<!-- grid.raster(img) -->
<!-- ``` -->

***

\huge

\begin{center}
R Primer
\end{center}



## Functions

\Large
```{r}
#| eval: false

function(arg1, arg2, ...){
  <the function code here...>
}
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| warning: false

sum(5, 2)
```
\pause 

```{r}
mean(1:4)
``` 

## Functions 

\Large
```{r}
#| eval: false

nchar("greetings")
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
nchar("greetings")
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| eval: false
ls()
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
ls()
```

## A Useful Function: `c()`


\Large 

To concatenate objects into a vector, use `c()`:
\pause 
```{r}
c(1, 3, 8, 20)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| eval: false
c("essex", "summer", "school")
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
c("essex", "summer", "school")
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
c(1, 2, "hello")
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
c(1, 2, "hello")
```

## Functions' Arguments


\LARGE
What arguments does a function have? \pause

```{r}
#| eval: false
help(median)
args(median)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
args(median)
```

## Functions' Arguments

\large
```{r}
median(1:3)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| results: hide
x <- c(1, 2, 3, NA) 
median(x)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
median(x)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
median(x, na.rm = TRUE)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
median(x, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## Functions' Arguments

\large

You can specify arguments in order or by name: \pause

```{r}
median(x, TRUE)
```
\pause 

```{r}
median(na.rm = TRUE, x)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| error: true
median(TRUE, x)
```


## Some Useful Functions

\large
Managing the workspace:

```{r}
# Get the working directory ("Where am I?"):
getwd()
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Set the working directory:
setwd("~/Desktop/")
```
\pause 

But there are better ways: `.Rproj` and `{here}` package


## Some Useful Functions

\large
Managing the workspace:

```{r}
# List objects in working dir:
ls()
# Remove `x' from working dir:
rm(x)
# Remove everything from working dir:
rm(list = ls())
```


## Some Useful Functions: Making vectors

```{r}
c(1, 2, 10)
```
\pause 
```{r}
1:4
6:3
```

## Some Useful Functions: Making vectors

\Large

```{r}
seq(from = 5, to = 30, by = 5)
```
\pause 
```{r}
rep(c("a", "b"), 2)
```
\pause 
```{r}
rep(c("a", "b"), each = 2)
```

## Extracting Elements from Vectors with `[`

\Large

```{r}
x <- sample(0:1, size = 10, replace = TRUE)
```

\pause 

```{r}
x
```

\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
x[3]
```

\pause 

```{r}
#| echo: false
x[3]
```


\pause 

```{r}
x[3] <- 999
```

\pause 
```{r}
x
```


## Some Useful Functions: Making lists

```{r}
v1 <- 1:8
v2 <- letters[1:5]
m <- matrix(1:9, 3, 3)
```

\pause 
```{r}
v1
v2
m
```

## Some Useful Functions: Making lists

```{r}
lll <- list(x = v1, y = v2, z = m)
```

\pause 

Extract by name:
```{r}
#| eval: false
lll$y
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
lll$y
```
\pause 

Extract by position with `[[`:
```{r}
#| eval: false
lll[[1]]
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
lll[[1]]
```
\pause 

Extract from sub-object: 
```{r}
#| eval: false
lll[[3]][2, 2]
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
lll[[3]][2, 2]
```



## Some Useful Mathematical Functions

\Large

```{r}
5 + 2
5 - 2
5 * 2
5 / 2
```

## Some Useful Mathematical Functions

\Large
```{r}
5 ^ 2
sqrt(25)
20 %% 3
```

## Some Useful Mathematical Functions and Values

```{r}
pi
abs(-3)
exp(1)
log(exp(2))
sin(pi / 2)
```

(See R Short Ref Card \ldots)



## Logicals

\large
```{r}
TRUE
```

```{r}
FALSE
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
TRUE == FALSE
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
TRUE == FALSE
```

 
## Logicals

\large
```{r}
#| eval: false
c(1, 2) == c(1, 3)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
c(1, 2) == c(1, 3)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
c(1, 2) != c(1, 3)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
c(1, 2) != c(1, 3)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false
c(1, 2) < c(1, 3)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
c(1, 2) < c(1, 3)
```

## Logicals

\large

```{r}
c(1, 2) > c(1, 3)
c(1, 2) <= c(1, 3)
c(1, 2) >= c(1, 3)
```

## How to Write a New Function

\Large
```{r}
sumDiff <- function(num1 = 3, num2 = 5){
  
  sum <- num1 + num2
  
  diff <- num1 - num2
  
  return(c(sum, diff))
}
```
\pause 
Now, cut and paste function into R prompt.

\pause 
(R will tell you if syntax error.)


## My New Function

\Large

```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff()
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false

sumDiff()
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff(3, 5)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false

sumDiff(3, 5)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff(num2 = 5, num1 = 3)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false

sumDiff(num2 = 5, num1 = 3)
```
\pause 

```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff(5, 3)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false

sumDiff(5, 3)
```

## My New Function

\Large
```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff(2, 20)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false

sumDiff(2, 20)
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| eval: false

sumDiff(1, "yes")
```
\pause 
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| error: true

sumDiff(1, "yes")
```





***

\huge

\begin{center}
Next:\\
Randomized Experiments, Estimation, and Inference\\ 
\pause 
\vspace{5mm}
Reading and PS1 for tomorrow!
\end{center}

\large
\begin{center}
Upload a {\tt .R} or {\tt .qmd} or {\tt .Rmd }or {\tt .txt} file to https://t.ly/1WuEn \\ (must be logged in)
\end{center}

***
